summary: I was born in Yugoslavia in 1915 and worked as a border policeman in the northern part, which is now Slovenia. During World War II, Yugoslavia was invaded by the Nazis coming down from Austria, which changed my life forever. After the war, Yugoslavia was under communist control, making it impossible for me to return home safely for many years. My experiences in Yugoslavia shaped my journey and the difficult choices I had to make.

During World War II, I served as a border policeman in Yugoslavia until the Nazis captured me in 1941. I was held as a prisoner of war in a labor camp in southern Germany until 1945. The war not only took me from my home but also led to my imprisonment and eventual displacement. It was the events of this war that ultimately led me to seek refuge and a new life in the United States.

The Nazis invaded Yugoslavia from Austria and captured me during World War II, making me a prisoner of war. They sent me to a forced labor camp in southern Germany where I was held from 1941 until early 1945. Their occupation disrupted my life and led to years of hardship and imprisonment. Being freed from the Nazis by the Third United States Army was a pivotal moment in my life.

I worked as a border policeman in the northern part of Yugoslavia, which today is known as Slovenia. This area was directly affected by the Nazi invasion coming from Austria during World War II. My role there put me in harm’s way and led to my capture and imprisonment. Slovenia represents the place where my life as a policeman and soldier began before the war changed everything.

The Nazis came down from Austria into Yugoslavia during World War II, which led to my capture. Austria was the launching point for the invasion that disrupted my life and forced me into captivity. This geographic detail is important because it shows how close the conflict was to my home. Austria’s role in the war directly impacted my fate as a prisoner of war.

After being captured by the Nazis, I was sent to a forced labor camp in southern Germany where I was held from 1941 until early 1945. Germany was where I endured the harsh conditions of imprisonment during the war. It was also in Germany that I was freed by the Third United States Army, which marked the beginning of my journey to freedom. However, even after liberation, I remained detained in the French-controlled zone of Germany as a displaced person.
cues: freed by Third United States Army

summary: The Third United States Army, led by General Patton, liberated me from the Nazi labor camp in early 1945. Their push back against the Nazis not only helped win the war but also gave me my freedom. This army was a symbol of hope and salvation during a dark time in my life. Their role was crucial in ending my imprisonment and starting my path toward a new life.

After being freed from the Nazis, I was detained again in the French-controlled zone of southern Germany. The French placed me in a displaced persons camp where I was held until they planned to send me back to Yugoslavia. This detention was difficult because returning to Yugoslavia meant danger due to the communist takeover. France’s control over this zone complicated my efforts to find true freedom.

After World War II, the Communist Party of Yugoslavia took control of the country, leading to political turmoil and mass killings of non-communist supporters like myself. This political shift made it dangerous for me to return home, which is why I escaped the displaced persons camp instead of going back. The communist regime’s rise was a major reason I sought refuge in the United States. Their control kept me away from my family farm for over a decade.

The United States became my new home after World War II when I immigrated as a displaced person in 1948. I was reassured that I would not be forced to return to communist Yugoslavia, which gave me hope. I contacted an aunt in Detroit who sponsored me, and I made my journey through Ellis Island. The United States offered me freedom and a fresh start after years of hardship and imprisonment.

Detroit, Michigan, is where I settled after arriving in the United States in 1948. I wrote to an aunt living there who sponsored my immigration. It was in Detroit that I married Mary Mrdjenovich and started a family with three children. Detroit became the place where I rebuilt my life after the war and displacement.

I entered the United States through Ellis Island in 1948 as a displaced person after World War II. Ellis Island was the gateway that marked the beginning of my new life in America. Passing through there was a significant moment, symbolizing my escape from war and political persecution. From Ellis Island, I traveled to Detroit to reunite with family and start over.
